question: Where are you from and where did you grow up?
answer: I'm from San Antonio, TX. I attended Judson High School, where I played both football and baseball.

question: What is your educational background?
answer:
I have two undergraduate degrees.

My first was awarded in 2016 in International Business, with a minor in Computer Science, from the University of the Incarnate Word (UIW) in San Antonio.

After several years in industry, I returned to school full-time in 2018 to pursue a second undergraduate degree in Electrical Engineering at the University of Texas at San Antonio (UTSA), with a focus in Digital Signal Processing. I also took several graduate-level Quantum Computing courses as electives — an area I'm genuinely passionate about.

While completing my EE degree I worked for two years at Southwest Research Institute (SwRI) as an embedded systems and signal processing engineer, leading or contributing to projects in firmware development, hardware/software integration, acoustic and audio system design, real-time DSP, and technical research.

The biggest accomplishment from that chapter was leading the electrical team for a sponsored Senior Design project that won first overall at the Fall 2021 UTSA Technology Symposium, along with a cash prize for the team.
query: educational background degrees UIW UTSA EE International Business

question: Did you play college sports?
answer: Yes — I played NCAA Division I football at UIW. I lined up at tight end, offensive tackle, and defensive end at different points in my career. Fun fact: when I was playing offensive tackle, I weighed up to 300 lbs. Another fun fact: Heisman Trophy winner and NFL running back Ricky Williams was my coach for two years at UIW.

question: What did you do between your first and second degrees?
answer: A few things. While studying at UIW I also worked as an HVAC technician on campus, servicing commercial equipment like chillers, boilers, and air handlers. After graduating with my business degree, I spent about two years designing commercial HVAC control systems in San Antonio and tech-led a small team of engineers before deciding to return to school full-time.

answer: I stay pretty active — running, cycling, and lifting are regular parts of my routine. I also really enjoy exploring craft coffee and wine, and I make a point of staying plugged into the latest developments in technology and science.

question: What are you working on now?
answer: I'm currently taking a deliberate break from my corporate career as a Senior Product Manager at National Instruments to focus on AI upskilling. I'm enrolled in the UT Post-Graduate Program in AI for Business Applications and working through the Ed Donner AI Curriculum — covering LLM Engineering, Agentic Engineering, MLOps, and more — toward the "Proficient AI Engineer" achievement.

question: What is your biggest professional accomplishment?
answer:
Leading four new product releases in my first two years as a Product Manager at National Instruments. Those releases drove a quarter-over-quarter ramp rate of 50% and generated a 15% lift to my portfolio's overall run-rate.

They were genuinely complex — requiring tight coordination across R&D, Sales, Marketing, Web Experience, S&OP, Manufacturing, and Systems. I also played a hands-on technical role, developing key IP, demos, and technical sales enablement along the way.

question: Tell me about a time you saved the company money or defended a revenue stream.
answer:
At NI, a critical component reached end-of-life on a singly-sourced ASIC due to a fab changeover that put key specifications — and potentially the finished product I managed — at risk.

I led a cross-functional team through the analysis and ultimately delivered a $3M bridge-buy recommendation that preserved a $30M revenue stream tied to a nearly $200M systems business. It required coordinating with S&OP, Procurement, Demand Planning, R&D, and Senior Leadership to balance cash outlay against supply security.
query: saved money defended revenue ASIC bridge-buy

question: Tell me about a time you managed a difficult situation.
answer:
The most challenging moment was pushing back on a directive from Senior Leadership to build a bloated, expensive product that would have served no target market particularly well.

I tied the core strategies of my product line to concepts that had already been validated with business units, then made the case — with data — that the proposed product had competing requirements, would be incredibly expensive and difficult to build, and that the company would be better served by releasing targeted products that attacked the broad base and vertical markets separately. It was a tense conversation, but the right one to have.

question: What sort of roles are you looking for?
answer: I'm a builder at heart. My ideal role lets me blend deep technical knowledge with product strategy to work on something genuinely meaningful — AI, space, advanced robotics, quantum computing. I thrive when I can flex both my engineering background and my business acumen. Product Management and Venture Capital roles tend to offer that mix, and those are the areas I'm focused on.
query: job search target roles looking for opportunities

question: What's your biggest strength?
answer: My ability to learn complicated, technical topics quickly and apply them directly to my role. I'm a systems thinker — I get real satisfaction from understanding how individual components work together to serve a larger purpose, and I use that perspective to make better product decisions.

question: What's your biggest weakness?
answer:
It's the flip side of my biggest strength. I love going deep on technical topics, and sometimes I go a bit *too* deep — which can make it harder to distill complex ideas for a less technical audience.

I've actively worked on this by adopting a goal-oriented approach: I ask what outcome I'm trying to serve and cut any detail that doesn't move toward it. It's made me a sharper communicator.

question: Why did you leave your last role, or why are you looking for new roles?
answer: Honestly, I felt like I had outgrown my position and was starting to stagnate — a combination of personal factors and organizational dynamics at NI. At the same time, my passion for AI was accelerating and I wanted a genuine new challenge. So I made a deliberate choice: step back, invest intensely in AI upskilling, and find that next career opportunity where I can make a bigger impact.

question: What is your most interesting project?
answer:
The most interesting project I've worked on is the **Non-Line-of-Sight (NLOS) Imaging with Acoustic Waves** research at SwRI (project 14-R6222), where I served as Principal Investigator.

The idea is essentially "seeing around corners" using sound waves instead of expensive pulsed lasers. Traditional NLOS imaging requires time-resolved photodetector systems that are costly and limited in range. Acoustic approaches offer a dramatically cheaper alternative that can capture hidden 3D geometry at longer ranges and shorter acquisition times.

The research drew on wave-propagation physics and signal processing techniques borrowed from seismic and radar imaging to reconstruct the position and shape of hidden objects from reflected acoustic signals.

**Key applications include:**
- Robotic and machine vision (autonomous vehicles navigating around obstacles)
- Remote sensing and surveillance
- Medical imaging
- Defense/stealth applications where active optical sensors are undesirable

More info: https://www.swri.org/what-we-do/internal-research-development/2022/electronicsautomation/non-line-of-sight-imaging-acoustic-waves-14-r6222
query: most interesting project NLOS acoustic imaging

question: What is your most technically challenging project?
answer:
The most technically demanding project was building a **real-time audio filtering system** on the STM32F7 Discovery board using a Vesper MEMS smart microphone and the ARM CMSIS-DSP library.

I led the full stack: hardware integration (STM32F7 + Wolfson WM8994 audio codec), DSP implementation using biquad cascade direct-form II transposed IIR filters with 32-bit floating point arithmetic, a touch-screen user interface for controlling a custom equalizer, and a MATLAB-based filter design workflow that generated coefficients (Butterworth, Chebyshev I/II, Elliptic) in the correct format for the ARM CMSIS-DSP framework.

The challenge was getting all of this working together in real time — audio in from the MEMS mic, processed through the MCU, and output without perceptible latency — while also giving the user live control over the equalizer parameters via the on-board LCD.
query: most technically challenging project DSP audio filtering ARM STM32

question: What project are you most proud of?
answer:
I'm most proud of my **Senior Design project at UTSA** — the ChickenMaxx 9000, a low-volume chicken seasoning machine prototype sponsored by HEB.

The system distributed seasoning from an elevated bin-hopper via a vibrator plate onto a conveyor belt below, powered by a 480V, 3-phase electrical system driving VFDs, conveyor motors, and the hopper vibrator.

I led the electrical sub-team across a two-team project (mechanical + electrical), which meant owning system design, customer communications with HEB, design reviews, and hands-on build participation — all while coordinating tightly with the mechanical team.

The result: **first overall at the Fall 2021 UTSA Technology Symposium** and a cash prize for the team. It was the most complex project I'd taken on at that point and the one that best demonstrated what I'm capable of when I'm leading end-to-end.
query: most proud of senior design ChickenMaxx UTSA
